steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The phrase 'Before that' signals that the blank describes a state that existed before the critic watched North by Northwest at last year's festival. When one past event precedes another past event, English requires the past perfect tense (had + past participle).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘is’: Simple present tense describes the current moment, not a prior past state. The sentence is set entirely in the past. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘had been’: Past perfect correctly marks that A Beautiful Mind was the least enjoyed film at a point before he watched North by Northwest (the later past event). ✓
  • Choice C — ‘was being’: Past progressive describes an action unfolding over time (e.g., 'the film was being screened'), not a completed prior state. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘has been’: Present perfect describes something that began in the past and continues into the present. The sentence is set entirely in the past, so present perfect is incorrect. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
